fact: A chunk of foam hit Columbia's wing during launch, punching a hole in the heat shield
source: Columbia Accident Investigation Board (CAIB) Report, Volume 1, NASA (2003)

fact: Engineers asked for satellite imagery to assess the damage but NASA management declined the request
source: Columbia Accident Investigation Board (CAIB) Report, Chapter 6: Decision Making at NASA

fact: Re-entry temperatures reached approximately 3,000 degrees (Fahrenheit) — the thermal protection system was the only barrier against re-entry plasma
source: NASA Space Shuttle Thermal Protection System overview; CAIB Report Chapter 3

fact: Columbia's mission (STS-107) lasted sixteen days in orbit before re-entry on February 1, 2003
Columbia broke apart over Texas during re-entry at approximately Mach 18, killing all seven crew members
source: Columbia Accident Investigation Board (CAIB) Report, Volume 1, Chapter 2 (2003)

fact: Engineers emailed concerns during the mission and managers closed the discussion, reasoning nothing could be done even if damage existed
source: CAIB Report, Chapter 6; also detailed in 'Comm Check: The Final Flight of Shuttle Columbia' by Michael Cabbage and William Harwood (2004)
